lines.translation: I was very busy and tired today.
I got up at 7:30 in the morning. After taking a shower, I had breakfast.
I ate while listening to the audio recording.
At 9:00, I went to the classroom for class.
The first class was Chinese. The teacher taught us pronunciation, new words, and grammar. She also taught us how to write characters and gave us a new text. The text was very interesting.
The second class was computer class. It was very difficult.
At noon, I went to the cafeteria with my classmates for lunch.
We practiced speaking Chinese while we ate.
In the afternoon, I went to the library to go online.
At 4:00, Wang Peng came to find me to play ball.
At 7:30, I went to Bai Ying'ai's dorm to chat with her.
When I got there, she was doing her homework.
Before I went to bed, Gao Wenzhong gave me a call. He told me there would be an exam tomorrow. I said I already knew.

example_sentence.cn: 第一节课是中文，老师教我们发音、生词和语法，也教我们写字，还给了我们一篇新课文，这篇课文很有意思。
example_sentence.py: Dì yī jié kè shì Zhōngwén, lǎoshī jiāo wǒmen fāyīn, shēngcí hé yǔfǎ, yě jiāo wǒmen xiě zì, hái gěile wǒmen yī piān xīn kèwén, zhè piān kèwén hěn yǒu yìsi.
example_sentence.translation: The first class was Chinese. The teacher taught us pronunciation, new words, and grammar. She also taught us how to write characters and gave us a new text. The text was very interesting.

example_sentence.cn: 今天我很忙，很累。
example_sentence.py: Jīntiān wǒ hěn máng, hěn lèi.
example_sentence.translation: I was very busy and tired today.

example_sentence.cn: 早上七点半起床，洗了澡以后就吃早饭。

example_sentence.translation: At 9:00, I went to the classroom for class.

example_sentence.py: Lǎoshī jiāo wǒmen fāyīn, shēngcí hé yǔfǎ.
example_sentence.translation: The teacher taught us pronunciation, new words, and grammar.

example_sentence.cn: 还给了我们一篇新课文，这篇课文很有意思。

example_sentence.translation: ...and gave us a new text. The text was very interesting.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: The usual colloquial term for a computer is 电脑 (diànnǎo), literally “electric brain.” A more formal term, especially in Mainland China, is 电子计算机 (diànzǐ jìsuànjī) (electronic computing machine) or simply 计算机 (jìsuànjī). But in Taiwan, 计算机 (jìsuànjī) means a calculator. In Mainland China, a calculator is called 计算器 (jìsuànqì).

grammar_sections.title: The position of time-when expressions
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Time-when expressions come before the verb. They often appear after the subject, but sometimes precede the subject under certain conditions. In this lesson, we focus on practicing time-when expressions positioned after the subject.

examples.hanzi: 我们十点上课。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen shí diǎn shàng kè.
examples.translation: We start class at ten.

examples.hanzi: 我们几点去？
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen jǐ diǎn qù?
examples.translation: What time are we going?

examples.hanzi: 你什么时候睡觉？
examples.pinyin: Nǐ shénme shíhou shuì jiào?
examples.translation: When do you go to bed?

examples.hanzi: 他明天上午八点来。
examples.pinyin: Tā míngtiān shàngwǔ bā diǎn lái.
examples.translation: He will come at eight tomorrow morning.

grammar_sections.title: The adverb 就 (jiù) (II)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: The adverb 就 (jiù) connects two verbs or verb phrases to indicate that the second action happens as soon as the first one is completed. [See also Grammar 3, Lesson 7.]

examples.hanzi: 他今天早上起床以后就听中文录音了。
examples.pinyin: Tā jīntiān zǎoshang qǐ chuáng yǐhòu jiù tīng Zhōngwén lùyīn le.
examples.translation: He listened to the Chinese audio right after he got up this morning.

examples.hanzi: 王朋看了电视以后就去睡觉了。
examples.pinyin: Wáng Péng kàn le diànshì yǐhòu jiù qù shuì jiào le.
examples.translation: Wang Peng went to bed right after watching TV.

examples.hanzi: 我做了功课以后就去朋友家玩儿。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuò le gōngkè yǐhòu jiù qù péngyou jiā wánr.
examples.translation: I will go to my friend's place right after I finish my homework.

grammar_sections.title: Describing simultaneity using 一边...一边... (yìbiān... yìbiān...)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: This structure describes two simultaneous actions. Typically, the first action is the principal one, which begins before the second, accompanying action.

patterns.pattern: Subject + 一边 + Verb Phrase 1, 一边 + Verb Phrase 2
patterns.pattern_pinyin: Subject + yìbiān + Verb Phrase 1, yìbiān + Verb Phrase 2
patterns.meaning_en: Used to describe two actions happening at the same time.

examples.hanzi: 我们一边吃晚饭,一边练习说中文。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen yìbiān chī wǎnfàn, yìbiān liànxí shuō Zhōngwén.
examples.translation: We practiced speaking Chinese while having dinner.

examples.hanzi: 他常常一边吃饭一边看电视。
examples.pinyin: Tā chángcháng yìbiān chī fàn yìbiān kàn diànshì.
examples.translation: He often eats and watches TV at the same time.

examples.hanzi: 我一边洗澡一边唱歌。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ yìbiān xǐ zǎo, yìbiān chàng gē.
examples.translation: I sang while I was showering.

examples.hanzi: 我妹妹喜欢一边看书一边听音乐。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ mèimei xǐhuan yìbiān kàn shū, yìbiān tīng yīnyuè.
examples.translation: My younger sister likes to listen to music while she reads.

grammar_sections.title: Series of verbs/verb phrases
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Some verbs and verb phrases can be used in succession to represent a series of actions. Their sequential order usually coincides with the temporal order of the actions.

examples.hanzi: 他常常去高小音家吃饭。
examples.pinyin: Tā chángcháng qù Gāo Xiǎoyīn jiā chī fàn.
examples.translation: He often goes to eat at Gao Xiaoyin's place.

examples.hanzi: 下午我要到图书馆去看书。
examples.pinyin: Xiàwǔ wǒ yào dào túshūguǎn qù kàn shū.
examples.translation: This afternoon, I will go to the library to read.

examples.hanzi: 我明天想找同学去打球。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ míngtiān xiǎng zhǎo tóngxué qù dǎ qiú.
examples.translation: I'd like to find some classmates to play ball with tomorrow.

examples.hanzi: 你明天来我家吃晚饭吧。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ míngtiān lái wǒ jiā chī wǎnfàn ba.
examples.translation: Come and have dinner at my house tomorrow.

grammar_sections.title: The particle 了 (le) (II)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: If a statement enumerates a series of realized actions or events, 了 (le) usually appears at the end of the series rather than after each verb. [See also Grammar 5, Lesson 5, and Grammar 2, Lesson 1, Volume 2.]

examples.hanzi: 昨天第一节课是中文。老师教我们发音、生词和语法,也教我们写字,还给了我们一篇新课文。那篇课文很有意思。
examples.pinyin: Zuótiān dì yī jié kè shì Zhōngwén. Lǎoshī jiāo wǒmen fāyīn, shēngcí hé yǔfǎ, yě jiāo wǒmen xiě zì, hái gěi le wǒmen yì piān xīn kèwén. Nà piān kèwén hěn yǒu yìsi.
examples.translation: Yesterday, the first class was Chinese. Our teacher taught us pronunciation, vocabulary, and grammar. She also taught us how to write characters and gave us a new text. That text was very interesting.

usage_notes: If a statement enumerates a series of realized actions or events, 了 (le) usually appears at the end of the series rather than after each verb.

grammar_sections.title: The particle 的 (de) (III)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: When a disyllabic or polysyllabic adjective modifies a noun, the particle 的 (de) is usually inserted between the adjective and the noun. However, with monosyllabic adjectives, 的 (de) is generally not required. If the adjective is preceded by 很 (hěn), however, 的 (de) cannot be dropped, e. g., 很大的教室 (hěn dà de jiàoshì) (very big classroom), 很好的老师 (hěn hǎo de lǎoshī) (very good teacher).

examples.hanzi: 漂亮的学校
examples.pinyin: piàoliang de xuéxiào
examples.translation: beautiful school

examples.hanzi: 容易的汉字
examples.pinyin: róngyì de Hànzì
examples.translation: easy character

examples.hanzi: 有意思的电影
examples.pinyin: yǒu yìsi de diànyǐng
examples.translation: interesting movie

examples.hanzi: 新课文
examples.pinyin: xīn kèwén
examples.translation: new text

examples.hanzi: 新电脑
examples.pinyin: xīn diànnǎo
examples.translation: new computer

examples.hanzi: 大教室
examples.pinyin: dà jiàoshì
examples.translation: big classroom

examples.hanzi: 好老师
examples.pinyin: hǎo lǎoshī
examples.translation: good teacher

examples.hanzi: 很大的教室
examples.pinyin: hěn dà de jiàoshì
examples.translation: very big classroom

examples.hanzi: 很好的老师
examples.pinyin: hěn hǎo de lǎoshī
examples.translation: very good teacher

usage_notes: When a disyllabic or polysyllabic adjective modifies a noun, the particle 的 (de) is usually inserted between the adjective and the noun.
With monosyllabic adjectives, 的 (de) is generally not required.
If the adjective is preceded by 很 (hěn), however, 的 (de) cannot be dropped.

grammar_sections.title: The 正在 (zhèngzài) v structure (be doing...)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: The 正在 (zhèngzài) v structure (be doing...) denotes an ongoing or progressive action at a certain point of time. It is more emphatic than 在 (zài), which can serve the same function.

patterns.meaning_en: Indicates an ongoing or progressive action.
Indicates an ongoing or progressive action (less emphatic than 正在).

examples.hanzi: Q: 李友, 你在做什么? A: 我在练习写汉字。
examples.pinyin: Q: Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ zài zuò shénme? A: Wǒ zài liànxí xiě Hànzì.
examples.translation: Q: Li You, what are you doing? A: I'm practicing writing Chinese characters.

examples.hanzi: 我们现在正在上课, 你别打电话。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xiànzài zhèngzài shàng kè, nǐ bié dǎ diànhuà.
examples.translation: We are in class right now. Don't make phone calls.

examples.hanzi: 我昨天到他宿舍的时候, 他正在练习发音。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān dào tā sùshè de shíhou, tā zhèngzài liànxí fāyīn.
examples.translation: When I got to his dorm yesterday, he was in the middle of practicing pronunciation.

examples.hanzi: Q: 你知道不知道王老师在哪儿? A: 他正在办公室开会。
examples.pinyin: Q: Nǐ zhīdao bù zhīdào Wáng lǎoshī zài nǎr? A: Tā zhèngzài bàngōngshì kāi huì.
examples.translation: Q: Do you know where Teacher Wang is? A: He is having a meeting in his office.

usage_notes: 正在 (zhèngzài) is more emphatic than 在 (zài), which can serve the same function.

global_config.visual_style: 现代、清新、生活化的风格，色彩明亮柔和，具有电影感。
global_config.setting_summary_cn: 一名叫李友的中国女大学生的日常生活，场景包括她的卧室、餐厅和教室。
global_config.setting_summary_en: The daily life of a female Chinese university student named Li You, with scenes in her bedroom, dining area, and classroom.

character_definitions.name: 李友
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A female Chinese university student in her early 20s, with shoulder-length black hair. She wears casual, comfortable home clothes like a t-shirt and sweatpants in the morning. She looks a bit tired but is diligent.

pinyin: shí yī yuè sān rì xīng qī èr
jīn tiān wǒ hěn máng hěn lèi
translation: I was very busy and tired today.

scenes.scene_goal: 开篇，通过李友写日记的疲惫神情，奠定“忙碌的一天”的基调。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 傍晚，李友坐在书桌前写日记。镜头特写日记本上的日期，然后拉远，看到她揉着眼睛，打了个哈欠，显得很疲惫。
scenes.setting_en: A cozy student dormitory room in the evening. A desk with a lamp, books, and an open diary.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Extreme close-up on a pen writing '十一月三日星期二' in a diary. The camera slowly pulls back to a medium shot of a tired-looking young Asian woman (Li You) sitting at her desk in a cozy dorm room. She sighs, rubs her eyes, and yawns, looking exhausted. The lighting is warm from a desk lamp.

voice_over.speaker: 李友
voice_over.hanzi: 十一月三日星期二。今天我很忙，很累。
voice_over.pinyin: shí yī yuè sān rì xīng qī èr. jīn tiān wǒ hěn máng hěn lèi.
voice_over.translation: November 3rd, Tuesday. I was very busy and tired today.

hanzi: 早上七点半起床，洗了澡以后就吃早饭。
pinyin: zǎo shang qī diǎn bàn qǐ chuáng xǐ le zǎo yǐ hòu jiù chī zǎo fàn
translation: I got up at 7:30 in the morning. After taking a shower, I had breakfast.

scenes.scene_goal: 通过快速剪辑展现李友早晨的忙碌流程，作为对日记内容的回忆。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 场景闪回至清晨。闹钟显示7:30，李友挣扎着起床。下一个镜头是她从浴室出来，用毛巾擦着湿漉漉的头发。最后她坐在餐桌前，面前摆着简单的早餐。
scenes.setting_en: A student dormitory room and small dining area in the early morning. Bright, natural light.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Flashback montage of a morning routine: 1. An alarm clock on a nightstand shows 7:30 AM, a hand slams it off. 2. Li You groggily gets out of bed, sunlight streaming through the window. 3. Li You emerges from the bathroom, toweling her damp hair. 4. Li You sits at a small table, with a simple breakfast of toast and milk. The cuts are quick to show she is in a hurry.

voice_over.speaker: 李友
voice_over.hanzi: 早上七点半起床，洗了澡以后就吃早饭。
voice_over.pinyin: zǎo shang qī diǎn bàn qǐ chuáng, xǐ le zǎo yǐ hòu jiù chī zǎo fàn.
voice_over.translation: I got up at 7:30 in the morning. After taking a shower, I had breakfast.

hanzi: 我一边吃饭，一边听录音。
pinyin: wǒ yìbiān chīfàn yìbiān tīng lùyīn
translation: I ate while listening to the audio recording.

scenes.scene_goal: 表现李友抓紧时间学习的勤奋状态。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 接上一个镜头，李友坐在餐桌前。她戴着耳机，面前的手机正在播放录音。她一边心不在焉地吃着面包，一边专注地听着，时不时还跟着录音小声跟读，表情认真。
scenes.setting_en: A small dining area in a student apartment or dorm. Morning light.
scenes.camera_movement: 从桌面上的早餐和手机，缓慢向上摇至她的脸部
scenes.video_prompt_en: Medium close-up shot of Li You at the breakfast table. She is wearing headphones connected to her smartphone, which is propped up on the table. She takes a bite of toast while listening intently, her eyes focused. She quietly mouths some words, following the audio recording. The camera tilts up from the phone to her focused face.

voice_over.speaker: 李友

scenes.scene_goal: 展示李友晚上的社交活动，并引出朋友正在学习的场景。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 晚上七点半，李友来到白英爱的宿舍门口，轻轻敲门。门打开后，她看到白英爱正戴着耳机，专注地在书桌前做功课，桌上堆着书本和笔记本。
scenes.setting_en: Interior of a cozy but slightly messy university dorm room in the evening. A desk is cluttered with books, a laptop, and stationery. The lighting is warm from a desk lamp.
scenes.camera_movement: Starts with a tracking shot following Li You down the hallway, then a static shot from inside the room as the door opens.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Slice-of-life anime style. A female student, Li You, knocks on a dorm room door. The door opens to reveal another female student, Bai Ying'ai, sitting at a desk piled with books, wearing headphones and studying intently under a desk lamp. A digital clock on the wall shows 7:30 PM. Li You smiles and waves gently.

voice_over.speaker: 李友 (日记旁白)
voice_over.hanzi: 七点半我去白英爱的宿舍跟她聊天。到那儿的时候，她正在做功课。

pinyin: wǒ bā diǎn bàn huí jiā

scenes.scene_goal: 表现李友结束社交，返回自己的空间。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 时钟指向八点半。李友和白英爱在门口挥手告别。下一个镜头，李友走在夜晚安静的校园小路上，路灯投下长长的影子。她回到了自己的宿舍，开门进去。
scenes.setting_en: University campus at night. Quiet pathways lit by streetlights. Then, the exterior and interior of Li You's own dorm room.
scenes.camera_movement: Pan from a clock showing 8:30 to Li You waving goodbye. Then a wide tracking shot of her walking on campus. Static shot as she enters her room.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Slice-of-life anime style. Transition shot of a clock showing 8:30 PM. Li You waves goodbye to her friend at a dorm room door. Cut to Li You walking alone on a peaceful, lamp-lit university campus path at night. She arrives at her own dorm room, unlocks the door, and walks inside.

hanzi: 睡觉以前，高文中给我打了一个电话，告诉我明天要考试，我说我已经知道了。

translation: Before I went to bed, Gao Wenzhong gave me a call. He told me there would be an exam tomorrow. I said I already knew.

scenes.scene_goal: 通过电话交代即将考试的信息，并展示李友已经准备好的状态。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 李友穿着睡衣，正准备上床睡觉。她的手机突然在床头柜上响起并振动。她拿起手机接听，屏幕上显示着“高文中”。她听着电话，表情平静，然后微笑着点点头，轻声回复。挂掉电话后，她看了一眼书桌上已经复习好的课本，自信地关掉了台灯。
scenes.setting_en: Interior of Li You's neat and tidy dorm room at night. She is in her pajamas, ready for bed.
scenes.camera_movement: Close-up on the vibrating phone, then a medium shot of Li You answering it. Zoom in slightly on her calm expression as she talks. Pan to her desk with textbooks before she turns off the light.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Slice-of-life anime style. In her cozy dorm room at night, Li You, dressed in pajamas, is about to sleep. Her smartphone on the nightstand buzzes and lights up with an incoming call from 'Gao Wenzhong'. She answers, listens calmly, nods, and speaks briefly with a confident smile. After hanging up, she glances at her neatly arranged textbooks on her desk, then turns off the lamp, plunging the room into darkness.

voice_over.speaker: 李友 (日记旁白)
voice_over.hanzi: 睡觉以前，高文中给我打了一个电话，告诉我明天要考试，我说我已经知道了。
voice_over.pinyin: shuìjiào yǐqián, Gāo Wénzhōng gěi wǒ dǎ le yí ge diànhuà, gàosu wǒ míngtiān yào kǎoshì, wǒ shuō wǒ yǐjīng zhīdào le.
voice_over.translation: Before I went to bed, Gao Wenzhong gave me a call. He told me there would be an exam tomorrow. I said I already knew.

global_config.teaching_style: Clear, structured, and encouraging, breaking down a diary entry to explain daily routines and time expressions in Mandarin.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, modern, with animated text and icons on digital flashcards.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to state the date, including month, day, and day of the week, following the Chinese convention.

narration.subtitle_en: The diary entry begins with the date. In Chinese, the date is written from the largest unit to the smallest: month, day, and then day of the week. So, [zh:十一月] is November, the 11th month. [zh:三日] means the 3rd day. And [zh:星期二] is Tuesday. This 'big-to-small' order is a very consistent and important rule in Chinese time expressions.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 十一月三日星期二
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shí yī yuè sān rì xīng qī èr
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: November 3rd, Tuesday
on_screen_text.notes: Date format: Month (月) + Day (日) + Day of the week (星期).

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'eleventh month'. Chinese months are formed by a number followed by 月 (yuè).
The character 日 (rì) means 'day' and is often used in written contexts like diaries. In spoken Chinese, 号 (hào) is more common.
Days of the week are formed by 星期 (xīngqī) followed by a number from one to six. Sunday is 星期天 (xīngqītiān) or 星期日 (xīngqīrì).

segments.visual_notes: Animate a calendar page flipping to November 3rd, Tuesday.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe one's state or feelings using the '很 + Adjective' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, Li You describes her day. She says, [zh:今天我很忙，很累]. [zh:今天] means 'today'. The core of the sentence is [zh:我很忙], 'I am very busy,' and [zh:很累], 'very tired.' The word [zh:很] is an adverb meaning 'very,' and it's very commonly placed before adjectives in Chinese to describe a state or feeling, even when the intensity isn't 'very' strong.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 今天我很忙，很累。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jīn tiān wǒ hěn máng hěn lèi
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I was very busy and tired today.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A time word that can be placed at the beginning of a sentence or after the subject.
An adjective used to describe a state of having much to do.
An adjective describing a feeling of weariness or lack of energy.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person juggling many tasks, then an icon of a person with low battery.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to describe a sequence of actions using '...以后, 就...'.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, Li You starts listing her morning activities in sequence. First, [zh:早上七点半起床] - 'get up at 7:30 in the morning.' Then, [zh:洗了澡以后就吃早饭] - 'after taking a shower, then had breakfast.' The structure 'Action 1 + [zh:以后] + [zh:就] + Action 2' is very common for showing that the second action happens right after the first one is completed.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 早上七点半起床，洗了澡以后就吃早饭。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zǎo shang qī diǎn bàn qǐ chuáng xǐ le zǎo yǐ hòu jiù chī zǎo fàn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I got up at 7:30 in the morning. After taking a shower, I had breakfast.
on_screen_text.notes: Sequence: Action 1 + 以后 (yǐhòu), 就 (jiù) + Action 2

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb-object phrase, literally 'to rise from bed'.
A verb-object phrase, literally 'to wash a bath'.
Used after a verb or clause to indicate that something happens after that action.
Literally 'morning meal'.

segments.visual_notes: Use arrow animations to show the flow from 'getting up' to 'showering' to 'eating breakfast'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn and understand the composition of key vocabulary for daily morning routines.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at the vocabulary for morning routines. [zh:起床] means 'to get up.' It's composed of [zh:起] 'to rise' and [zh:床] 'bed.' So, literally 'rise from bed.' [zh:洗澡] means 'to take a bath or shower,' combining [zh:洗] 'to wash' and [zh:澡] 'bath.' Finally, [zh:早饭] is 'breakfast,' made of [zh:早] 'morning' and [zh:饭] 'meal.' Understanding these components can help you remember the words.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 起床 / 洗澡 / 早饭
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: qǐ chuáng / xǐ zǎo / zǎo fàn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: to get up / to take a shower / breakfast

highlight_words.explanation_en: To rise from bed.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 起 (qǐ) means 'to rise' + 床 (chuáng) means 'bed'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The action of washing one's body.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 洗 (xǐ) means 'to wash' + 澡 (zǎo) means 'bath'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The first meal of the day.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 早 (zǎo) means 'early' or 'morning' + 饭 (fàn) means 'meal' or 'rice'.

segments.visual_notes: For each word, show the characters separately with their meanings, then combine them into the full word.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '一边...一边...' structure for describing simultaneous actions.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence introduces a useful pattern for describing two actions happening at the same time: [zh:一边...一边...]. Li You says, [zh:我一边吃饭，一边听录音]. This means 'I ate while listening to the recording.' The first action, [zh:吃饭] 'to eat a meal,' is typically the main one, and the second, [zh:听录音] 'listen to a recording,' is the accompanying one.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我一边吃饭，一边听录音。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ yìbiān chīfàn yìbiān tīng lùyīn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I ate while listening to the audio recording.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure for simultaneous actions: 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 1, 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 2

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used in pairs to connect two simultaneous actions.
A general term for eating, not specific to breakfast, lunch, or dinner.
Literally 'to record sound', but here it refers to the recording itself.

segments.visual_notes: Split the screen to show someone eating on one side and listening with headphones on the other.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '到 + Place + 去 + Action' structure for describing going somewhere to do something.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence describes a series of actions leading to a goal. [zh:九点到教室去上课]. It means 'At 9:00, I went to the classroom for class.' The structure is 'Time + [zh:到] Place + [zh:去] Action.' [zh:到] means 'to arrive at,' [zh:教室] is 'classroom,' and [zh:上课] means 'to attend class.' The sequence of verbs reflects the actual order of events.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 九点到教室去上课。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jiǔ diǎn dào jiàoshì qù shàngkè
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: At 9:00, I went to the classroom for class.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure: 到 (dào) + Place + 去 (qù) + Action

highlight_words.explanation_en: Composed of 教 (jiāo) 'to teach' and 室 (shì) 'room'.

highlight_words.translation: to go to class; to attend class
highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb-object phrase, literally 'to go up to class'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character walking from a clock showing 9:00 to a building labeled '教室'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a longer, descriptive sentence about class activities and learn related vocabulary.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a long sentence describing Li You's Chinese class. It starts with [zh:第一节课是中文], 'The first period class was Chinese.' Then it lists what the teacher taught: [zh:发音] (pronunciation), [zh:生词] (new words), and [zh:语法] (grammar). The teacher also taught them [zh:写字] (to write characters) and gave them a [zh:新课文] (new text). The sentence ends with a comment: [zh:这篇课文很有意思], 'This text is very interesting.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 第一节课是中文，老师教我们发音、生词和语法，也教我们写字，还给了我们一篇新课文，这篇课文很有意思。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: dì yī jié kè shì zhōngwén lǎoshī jiāo wǒmen fāyīn shēngcí hé yǔfǎ yě jiāo wǒmen xiězì hái gěi le wǒmen yì piān xīn kèwén zhè piān kèwén hěn yǒu yìsi
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The first class was Chinese. The teacher taught us pronunciation, new words, and grammar. She also taught us how to write characters and gave us a new text. The text was very interesting.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to count periods of a class, e.g., 第一节课 (dì yī jié kè).
The verb for the action of teaching.
Used for texts, articles, or essays, like in 一篇课文 (yì piān kèwén).
Literally 'has meaning', used to describe something as interesting or fun.

segments.visual_notes: Use icons to represent each element taught: pronunciation (sound waves), new words (flashcard), grammar (gears), writing (a pen).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn essential vocabulary for talking about studying a language.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on the vocabulary for language learning. [zh:发音] means 'pronunciation.' [zh:生词] means 'new words'; [zh:生] means 'new' or 'unfamiliar' here. [zh:语法] is 'grammar.' And [zh:课文] is the 'text' in a textbook. These are essential words for any Chinese student to know when talking about their studies.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 发音 / 生词 / 语法 / 课文
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: fāyīn / shēngcí / yǔfǎ / kèwén
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: pronunciation / new words / grammar / text

highlight_words.explanation_en: The way a word or a language is spoken.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 发 (fā) means 'to issue' + 音 (yīn) means 'sound'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Words that one is not yet familiar with.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 生 (shēng) means 'unfamiliar' + 词 (cí) means 'word'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The rules of a language.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 语 (yǔ) means 'language' + 法 (fǎ) means 'rule' or 'law'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The main body of a lesson in a textbook.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 课 (kè) means 'lesson' + 文 (wén) means 'text'.

segments.visual_notes: Display each word with a relevant icon and its character breakdown.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe a class and give a simple opinion about it.

narration.subtitle_en: After Chinese class, Li You had computer class. The sentence is [zh:第二节是电脑课，很难]. [zh:第二节] means 'the second period.' [zh:电脑课] is 'computer class.' The word [zh:电脑] literally means 'electric brain.' Finally, she gives her opinion on the class: [zh:很难], which means 'very difficult.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 第二节是电脑课，很难。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: dì'èr jié shì diànnǎo kè hěn nán
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The second class was computer class. It was very difficult.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A compound word from 电 (diàn) 'electric' and 脑 (nǎo) 'brain'.
Refers to a course or a single lesson.
An adjective used to describe something that is hard to do or understand.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon for a computer, followed by a thumbs-down or a confused face icon to represent 'difficult'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to describe doing an activity with other people using '和...一起'.

narration.subtitle_en: Now it's lunchtime. [zh:中午我和同学们一起到餐厅去吃午饭]. [zh:中午] means 'noon.' The phrase [zh:和同学们一起] means 'together with classmates.' The structure is similar to what we saw before: they go [zh:到餐厅] 'to the cafeteria' [zh:去吃午饭] 'to eat lunch.' This sentence emphasizes doing an activity as a group.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 中午我和同学们一起到餐厅去吃午饭。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhōngwǔ wǒ hé tóngxué men yìqǐ dào cāntīng qù chī wǔfàn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: At noon, I went to the cafeteria with my classmates for lunch.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure for group activities: 和 (hé) + People + 一起 (yìqǐ)

highlight_words.explanation_en: The middle of the day.
Literally 'same study'.
Used to indicate that an action is performed by two or more people jointly.
A place for eating meals.
Literally 'noon meal'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a group of friends walking together towards a building labeled '餐厅'.

segments.teaching_goal: To reinforce the use of the '一边...一边...' structure for simultaneous actions.

narration.subtitle_en: Here we see the [zh:一边...一边...] structure again, reinforcing how to talk about doing two things at once. [zh:我们一边吃，一边练习说中文]. This means 'We ate while practicing speaking Chinese.' It shows how they use their lunchtime productively to improve their language skills. The verb [zh:练习] means 'to practice.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我们一边吃，一边练习说中文。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒmen yìbiān chī yìbiān liànxí shuō zhōngwén
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: We practiced speaking Chinese while we ate.
on_screen_text.notes: Reinforcing: 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 1, 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 2

highlight_words.explanation_en: The action of repeatedly doing something to become skilled.
The verb for speaking.

segments.visual_notes: Show friends at a lunch table with speech bubbles containing Chinese characters.

segments.teaching_goal: To practice describing afternoon activities using the '到 + Place + 去 + Action' pattern.

narration.subtitle_en: In the afternoon, Li You's activities continue. She says, [zh:下午我到图书馆去上网]. [zh:下午] means 'afternoon.' Following the 'go to a place to do something' pattern, she went [zh:到图书馆] 'to the library' [zh:去上网] 'to go online.' The verb [zh:上网] is a very common phrase that literally means 'to get on the net.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 下午我到图书馆去上网。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xiàwǔ wǒ dào túshūguǎn qù shàngwǎng
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: In the afternoon, I went to the library to go online.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The part of the day between noon and evening.
A place where books and other materials are available for people to use or borrow.
A verb-object phrase, literally 'to get on the net'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character walking to a library icon, then sitting at a computer with a Wi-Fi symbol.

segments.segment_title: A Friend Comes to Play Ball
segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to describe someone coming to find you for an activity using a series of verbs.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence describes a friend's visit. [zh:四点王朋来找我打球]. At [zh:四点], 'four o'clock,' her friend Wang Peng [zh:来] 'came' [zh:找我] 'to find me' [zh:打球] 'to play ball.' The series of verbs [zh:来], [zh:找], and [zh:打球] shows the purpose of his visit in a very direct and logical sequence, which is common in Chinese.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 四点王朋来找我打球。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: sì diǎn Wáng Péng lái zhǎo wǒ dǎqiú
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: At 4:00, Wang Peng came to find me to play ball.
on_screen_text.notes: A series of verbs can show a sequence of actions or purpose.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates movement towards the speaker.
The action of seeking someone or something.
A general term for playing ball games, like basketball, tennis, etc.

segments.visual_notes: Show a character (Wang Peng) walking towards another character (Li You) with a basketball.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to tell time using '刻' (kè) for quarter-hours.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence tells us about dinner time. [zh:五点三刻吃晚饭]. This means 'had dinner at 5:45.' [zh:五点] is 'five o'clock.' The word [zh:刻] means 'a quarter of an hour.' So, [zh:三刻] means 'three quarters,' or 45 minutes past the hour. This is a common way to express time in Chinese, alongside saying the exact minutes.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 五点三刻吃晚饭。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǔ diǎn sān kè chī wǎnfàn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I had dinner at 5:45.
on_screen_text.notes: Telling time: 一刻 (yī kè) = 15 min, 三刻 (sān kè) = 45 min.

highlight_words.translation: three quarters of an hour; 45 minutes
highlight_words.explanation_en: A way to express 45 minutes past the hour. 刻 (kè) means a 15-minute interval.
Literally 'evening meal'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an analog clock with the hands moving to 5:45, highlighting the three 15-minute segments.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe going to someone's place to do an activity with them.

narration.subtitle_en: In the evening, Li You visits a friend. [zh:七点半我去白英爱的宿舍跟她聊天]. At [zh:七点半], '7:30,' she went to Bai Ying'ai's [zh:宿舍], which means 'dormitory.' The purpose was [zh:跟她聊天], 'to chat with her.' The word [zh:聊天] is a very common term for having a casual conversation or chat.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 七点半我去白英爱的宿舍跟她聊天（儿）。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: qī diǎn bàn wǒ qù Bái Yīng'ài de sùshè gēn tā liáotiān er
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: At 7:30, I went to Bai Ying'ai's dorm to chat with her.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Living quarters for students at a school or university.
To have an informal conversation. The 'er' at the end is a common suffix in northern Chinese dialects that softens the tone.

segments.visual_notes: Show a character walking to a dorm room, where another character is waiting with a speech bubble.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '正在' structure to describe an action in progress at a specific time.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence describes what her friend was doing when she arrived. [zh:到那儿的时候，她正在做功课]. The phrase [zh:到那儿的时候] means 'when I got there.' The key structure here is [zh:正在], which indicates an ongoing, progressive action. So, [zh:她正在做功课] means 'she was in the middle of doing homework.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 到那儿的时候，她正在做功课。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: dào nàr de shíhou tā zhèngzài zuò gōngkè
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: When I got there, she was doing her homework.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure for ongoing action: 正在 (zhèngzài) + Verb

highlight_words.explanation_en: A phrase that follows a verb or clause to mean 'at the time when...'
An adverb that emphasizes an action is in progress.
Academic work that students are required to do at home.

segments.visual_notes: Show a character at a desk writing, with 'progress' lines animating around them to indicate an ongoing action.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the simple and common phrase for returning home.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a simple, straightforward sentence about returning home. [zh:我八点半回家]. It means 'I went home at 8:30.' [zh:八点半] is 'eight-thirty.' The verb [zh:回家] means 'to return home,' combining [zh:回] 'to return' and [zh:家] 'home.' It's a very common and useful verb for daily conversation.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我八点半回家。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ bā diǎn bàn huí jiā
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I went home at 8:30.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb-object phrase meaning to return to one's home.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 回 (huí) means 'to return' + 家 (jiā) means 'home'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a character walking towards a house icon under a night sky.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a complex sentence involving time sequence, a phone call, and reported speech.

narration.subtitle_en: The diary entry concludes with an event before bedtime. [zh:睡觉以前] means 'before sleeping.' Gao Wenzhong [zh:给我打了一个电话], 'gave me a phone call.' He [zh:告诉] 'told' her that [zh:明天要考试], 'there will be an exam tomorrow.' Her response was [zh:我说我已经知道了], 'I said I already knew.' This sentence shows how to report what someone said and describes a sequence of events.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 睡觉以前，高文中给我打了一个电话，告诉我明天要考试，我说我已经知道了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shuìjiào yǐqián Gāo Wénzhōng gěi wǒ dǎ le yí ge diànhuà gàosu wǒ míngtiān yào kǎoshì wǒ shuō wǒ yǐjīng zhīdào le
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Before I went to bed, Gao Wenzhong gave me a call. He told me there would be an exam tomorrow. I said I already knew.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The action of sleeping.
Used after a verb or clause to indicate something happened before that event.
Literally 'electric speech'.
The verb for informing someone of something.
A test of a student's knowledge or skill.
An adverb indicating that an action has already happened.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a sequence: a character by a bed, a phone ringing, a calendar showing 'Exam Tomorrow', and finally a speech bubble with 'I know'.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students that time expressions in Chinese come before the verb, usually after the subject.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's start with our first grammar point: where to place time expressions. In English, you might say 'I get up at 7:30.' But in Chinese, the time expression must come before the verb. The most common structure is Subject, then Time, then the Verb. For example, in the diary, Li You says [zh:早上七点半起床], which means 'in the morning, 7:30, get up.' Notice how the time comes before the action. Another example is [zh:九点到教室去上课], 'at 9:00, go to the classroom for class.' Always remember: time first, then action.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我们十点上课。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒmen shí diǎn shàng kè.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: We start class at ten.

grammar_points.pattern: 我们十点上课。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen shí diǎn shàng kè.
grammar_points.explanation_en: The time expression '十点' (shí diǎn) comes after the subject '我们' (wǒmen) and before the verb phrase '上课' (shàng kè).

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 我们几点去？
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen jǐ diǎn qù?
grammar_points.explanation_en: When asking a question about time, the question word '几点' (jǐ diǎn) also goes before the verb '去' (qù).

grammar_points.pattern: 他明天上午八点来。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā míngtiān shàngwǔ bā diǎn lái.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Longer time expressions like '明天上午八点' (míngtiān shàngwǔ bā diǎn) also follow this rule, placed before the verb '来' (lái).

segments.visual_notes: Animate the sentence structure on screen: [Subject] [Time] [Verb]. Show the English and Chinese sentences side-by-side to highlight the word order difference.

segments.teaching_goal: To explain how 就 (jiù) connects two actions, indicating the second happens immediately after the first.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at the adverb [zh:就]. This is a very useful word that indicates that a second action happens right after the first one is completed. In the diary, Li You writes [zh:洗了澡以后就吃早饭]. This means 'after taking a shower, THEN I had breakfast.' The word [zh:就] creates a strong connection between the two events, emphasizing their immediacy. The pattern is typically 'first action, [zh:以后], [zh:就], second action.' It shows a smooth and quick transition from one activity to the next.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他今天早上起床以后就听中文录音了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tā jīntiān zǎoshang qǐ chuáng yǐhòu jiù tīng Zhōngwén lùyīn le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: He listened to the Chinese audio right after he got up this morning.
on_screen_text.notes: Pattern: Action 1 + 以后 (yǐhòu) + 就 (jiù) + Action 2

grammar_points.pattern: 王朋看了电视以后就去睡觉了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wáng Péng kàn le diànshì yǐhòu jiù qù shuì jiào le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Wang Peng went to bed right after watching TV. '就' connects watching TV and going to bed.

grammar_points.pattern: 我做了功课以后就去朋友家玩儿。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ zuò le gōngkè yǐhòu jiù qù péngyou jiā wánr.
grammar_points.explanation_en: I will go to my friend's place right after I finish my homework. '就' indicates the immediate plan after finishing homework.

segments.visual_notes: Visually link two action icons with an arrow labeled '就 (jiù)' to show immediate succession.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Simultaneous Actions with 一边...一边...
segments.teaching_goal: To teach the 'yìbiān... yìbiān...' structure for describing two actions happening at the same time.

narration.subtitle_en: How do you say you're doing two things at once? You use the structure [zh:一边]...[zh:一边]. This is equivalent to saying 'while' or 'at the same time' in English. Li You uses it twice in her diary. First, she says [zh:我一边吃饭，一边听录音], 'I ate while listening to the recording.' Later, she says [zh:我们一边吃，一边练习说中文], 'We ate while practicing speaking Chinese.' The structure is simple: Subject, then [zh:一边] plus the first action, followed by [zh:一边] and the second action. It's perfect for describing multitasking!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他常常一边吃饭一边看电视。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tā chángcháng yìbiān chī fàn yìbiān kàn diànshì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: He often eats and watches TV at the same time.
on_screen_text.notes: Pattern: Subject + 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 1, 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 2

grammar_points.pattern: 我一边洗澡一边唱歌。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ yìbiān xǐ zǎo, yìbiān chàng gē.
grammar_points.explanation_en: I sang while I was showering. Both actions happen simultaneously.

grammar_points.pattern: 我妹妹喜欢一边看书一边听音乐。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ mèimei xǐhuan yìbiān kàn shū, yìbiān tīng yīnyuè.
grammar_points.explanation_en: My younger sister likes to listen to music while she reads. This describes a habitual preference for doing two things at once.

segments.visual_notes: Use a split-screen effect to show two actions happening at the same time, with '一边' labels on each side.

segments.teaching_goal: To explain how to string verbs together to describe a sequence of actions, where the order of verbs matches the order of actions.

narration.subtitle_en: In Chinese, you can often list a series of actions by simply putting the verbs one after another, without needing words like 'and' or 'to'. The order of the verbs follows the natural sequence of the events. For instance, Li You says she will [zh:到图书馆去上网], which literally means 'arrive at the library, go, and surf the web.' The verbs [zh:到], [zh:去], and [zh:上网] are linked together to describe the whole process. Another example is [zh:王朋来找我打球], 'Wang Peng came to find me to play ball.' The actions happen in that exact order: come, find, play.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 下午我要到图书馆去看书。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xiàwǔ wǒ yào dào túshūguǎn qù kàn shū.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This afternoon, I will go to the library to read.
on_screen_text.notes: The verbs are ordered chronologically: 到 (arrive), 去 (go), 看 (read).

grammar_points.pattern: 他常常去高小音家吃饭。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā chángcháng qù Gāo Xiǎoyīn jiā chī fàn.
grammar_points.explanation_en: He often goes to Gao Xiaoyin's place to eat. The sequence is 'go' then 'eat'.

grammar_points.pattern: 我明天想找同学去打球。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ míngtiān xiǎng zhǎo tóngxué qù dǎ qiú.
grammar_points.explanation_en: I'd like to find some classmates to play ball with tomorrow. The sequence is 'find classmates', 'go', then 'play ball'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a sequence of icons representing the actions, connected by arrows to emphasize the temporal order.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: The Particle 了 (le) in a Series
segments.teaching_goal: To teach that when listing a series of completed actions, 了 (le) is usually placed only after the final verb in the series.

narration.subtitle_en: We've learned that [zh:了] can mark a completed action. But what happens when you have a list of completed actions? In Chinese, you typically don't put [zh:了] after every single verb. Instead, you place it at the end of the series. Look at this long sentence from the diary describing the Chinese class. The teacher taught pronunciation, new words, grammar, and how to write characters. Finally, she 'gave' them a new text. The particle [zh:了] appears after [zh:给], the final action in that list: [zh:还给了我们一篇新课文]. This makes the sentence flow much more naturally than repeating [zh:了] over and over.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 老师教我们发音、生词和语法，也教我们写字，还给了我们一篇新课文。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǎoshī jiāo wǒmen fāyīn, shēngcí hé yǔfǎ, yě jiāo wǒmen xiě zì, hái gěi le wǒmen yì piān xīn kèwén.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The teacher taught us pronunciation, vocabulary, and grammar, also taught us to write characters, and also gave us a new text.
on_screen_text.notes: Notice '了' (le) appears only after the final verb in the series, '给' (gěi).

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the verbs in the sentence. Show '了' appearing only after the last verb, while the others remain without it.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: The Particle 的 (de) with Adjectives
segments.teaching_goal: To explain when to use 的 (de) between an adjective and a noun.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's discuss the particle [zh:的], which is often used to connect an adjective to a noun. The rule depends on the adjective. For adjectives with two or more syllables, like [zh:有意思] (interesting), you usually need [zh:的]. For example, [zh:有意思的电影] means 'an interesting movie.' However, for single-syllable adjectives like [zh:新] (new), you often don't need [zh:的]. In the text, we see [zh:新课文], 'new text.' But there's a catch! If you add [zh:很] (very) before the adjective, you must use [zh:的], even for single-syllable ones. For example, you would say [zh:很大的教室] for 'a very big classroom'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 漂亮的学校 / 新课文 / 很大的教室
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: piàoliang de xuéxiào / xīn kèwén / hěn dà de jiàoshì
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: beautiful school / new text / very big classroom
on_screen_text.notes: Usage of 的 (de) depends on the adjective's length and the presence of 很 (hěn).

grammar_points.pattern: 漂亮的学校
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: piàoliang de xuéxiào
grammar_points.explanation_en: Use '的' with a two-syllable adjective like '漂亮' (piàoliang).

grammar_points.pattern: 新课文
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: xīn kèwén
grammar_points.explanation_en: Omit '的' with a one-syllable adjective like '新' (xīn).

grammar_points.pattern: 很大的教室
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: hěn dà de jiàoshì
grammar_points.explanation_en: Must use '的' when '很' (hěn) precedes the adjective, even a one-syllable one like '大' (dà).

segments.visual_notes: Show three columns illustrating the rules: '2+ Syllable Adj + 的 + Noun', '1-Syllable Adj + Noun', and '很 + Adj + 的 + Noun'.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Ongoing Actions with 正在 (zhèngzài)
segments.teaching_goal: To teach the 'zhèngzài + verb' structure to indicate an ongoing action, and its relationship with 在 (zài).

narration.subtitle_en: Our final grammar point is about describing an action that is currently in progress. To do this, you use the word [zh:正在] before the verb. It's like adding '-ing' to a verb in English. In the diary, when Li You arrives at Bai Ying'ai's dorm, she finds that [zh:她正在做功课], 'She was in the middle of doing her homework.' The word [zh:正在] emphasizes that the action is happening right at that moment. You can also use just [zh:在] for the same purpose, but [zh:正在] is a bit more emphatic, really stressing the 'right now' aspect of the action.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我昨天到他宿舍的时候，他正在练习发音。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān dào tā sùshè de shíhou, tā zhèngzài liànxí fāyīn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: When I got to his dorm yesterday, he was in the middle of practicing pronunciation.
on_screen_text.notes: 正在 (zhèngzài) + Verb = an action in progress.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 李友，你在做什么？ A: 我在练习写汉字。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ zài zuò shénme? Wǒ zài liànxí xiě Hànzì.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Here, '在' (zài) is used to indicate an ongoing action. It's slightly less emphatic than '正在' (zhèngzài).

grammar_points.pattern: 我们现在正在上课，你别打电话。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen xiànzài zhèngzài shàng kè, nǐ bié dǎ diànhuà.
grammar_points.explanation_en: '正在' (zhèngzài) strongly emphasizes that the class is happening right now.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a 'loading' or 'in progress' icon next to the verb when '正在' is on screen to visually represent the ongoing action.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key grammar patterns and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned in this lesson. We talked about placing time expressions before the verb, using [zh:就] for sequential actions, and [zh:一边...一边] for simultaneous actions. We also covered how to use [zh:了] in a series of verbs and when to use [zh:的] with adjectives. Finally, we learned to describe ongoing actions with [zh:正在]. Keep practicing these patterns, and you'll be able to describe your day in Chinese just like Li You!

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to state the date, including month, day, and day of the week, following the Chinese convention.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The diary entry begins with the date. In Chinese, the date is written from the largest unit to the smallest: month, day, and then day of the week. So, [zh:十一月] is November, the 11th month. [zh:三日] means the 3rd day. And [zh:星期二] is Tuesday. This 'big-to-small' order is a very consistent and important rule in Chinese time expressions.

content.focus_text: 十一月三日星期二
content.focus_pinyin: shí yī yuè sān rì xīng qī èr
content.focus_gloss_en: November 3rd, Tuesday

content.notes: Date format: Month (月) + Day (日) + Day of the week (星期).

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'eleventh month'. Chinese months are formed by a number followed by 月 (yuè).
The character 日 (rì) means 'day' and is often used in written contexts like diaries. In spoken Chinese, 号 (hào) is more common.
Days of the week are formed by 星期 (xīngqī) followed by a number from one to six. Sunday is 星期天 (xīngqītiān) or 星期日 (xīngqīrì).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe one's state or feelings using the '很 + Adjective' structure.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Next, Li You describes her day. She says, [zh:今天我很忙，很累]. [zh:今天] means 'today'. The core of the sentence is [zh:我很忙], 'I am very busy,' and [zh:很累], 'very tired.' The word [zh:很] is an adverb meaning 'very,' and it's very commonly placed before adjectives in Chinese to describe a state or feeling, even when the intensity isn't 'very' strong.

content.focus_text: 今天我很忙，很累。
content.focus_pinyin: jīn tiān wǒ hěn máng hěn lèi
content.focus_gloss_en: I was very busy and tired today.

An adjective used to describe a state of having much to do.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to describe a sequence of actions using '...以后, 就...'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here, Li You starts listing her morning activities in sequence. First, [zh:早上七点半起床] - 'get up at 7:30 in the morning.' Then, [zh:洗了澡以后就吃早饭] - 'after taking a shower, then had breakfast.' The structure 'Action 1 + [zh:以后] + [zh:就] + Action 2' is very common for showing that the second action happens right after the first one is completed.

content.focus_text: 早上七点半起床，洗了澡以后就吃早饭。
content.focus_pinyin: zǎo shang qī diǎn bàn qǐ chuáng xǐ le zǎo yǐ hòu jiù chī zǎo fàn

A verb-object phrase, literally 'to wash a bath'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn and understand the composition of key vocabulary for daily morning routines.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at the vocabulary for morning routines. [zh:起床] means 'to get up.' It's composed of [zh:起] 'to rise' and [zh:床] 'bed.' So, literally 'rise from bed.' [zh:洗澡] means 'to take a bath or shower,' combining [zh:洗] 'to wash' and [zh:澡] 'bath.' Finally, [zh:早饭] is 'breakfast,' made of [zh:早] 'morning' and [zh:饭] 'meal.' Understanding these components can help you remember the words.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '一边...一边...' structure for describing simultaneous actions.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This sentence introduces a useful pattern for describing two actions happening at the same time: [zh:一边...一边...]. Li You says, [zh:我一边吃饭，一边听录音]. This means 'I ate while listening to the recording.' The first action, [zh:吃饭] 'to eat a meal,' is typically the main one, and the second, [zh:听录音] 'listen to a recording,' is the accompanying one.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '到 + Place + 去 + Action' structure for describing going somewhere to do something.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This sentence describes a series of actions leading to a goal. [zh:九点到教室去上课]. It means 'At 9:00, I went to the classroom for class.' The structure is 'Time + [zh:到] Place + [zh:去] Action.' [zh:到] means 'to arrive at,' [zh:教室] is 'classroom,' and [zh:上课] means 'to attend class.' The sequence of verbs reflects the actual order of events.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students that time expressions in Chinese come before the verb, usually after the subject.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's start with our first grammar point: where to place time expressions. In English, you might say 'I get up at 7:30.' But in Chinese, the time expression must come before the verb. The most common structure is Subject, then Time, then the Verb. For example, in the diary, Li You says [zh:早上七点半起床], which means 'in the morning, 7:30, get up.' Notice how the time comes before the action. Another example is [zh:九点到教室去上课], 'at 9:00, go to the classroom for class.' Always remember: time first, then action.

content.focus_text: 我们十点上课。
content.focus_pinyin: Wǒmen shí diǎn shàng kè.
content.focus_gloss_en: We start class at ten.

grammar_points.pattern: 我们十点上课。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen shí diǎn shàng kè.
grammar_points.explanation_en: The time expression '十点' (shí diǎn) comes after the subject '我们' (wǒmen) and before the verb phrase '上课' (shàng kè).

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 我们几点去？
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen jǐ diǎn qù?
grammar_points.explanation_en: When asking a question about time, the question word '几点' (jǐ diǎn) also goes before the verb '去' (qù).

grammar_points.pattern: 他明天上午八点来。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā míngtiān shàngwǔ bā diǎn lái.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Longer time expressions like '明天上午八点' (míngtiān shàngwǔ bā diǎn) also follow this rule, placed before the verb '来' (lái).

segments.teaching_goal: To explain how 就 (jiù) connects two actions, indicating the second happens immediately after the first.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at the adverb [zh:就]. This is a very useful word that indicates that a second action happens right after the first one is completed. In the diary, Li You writes [zh:洗了澡以后就吃早饭]. This means 'after taking a shower, THEN I had breakfast.' The word [zh:就] creates a strong connection between the two events, emphasizing their immediacy. The pattern is typically 'first action, [zh:以后], [zh:就], second action.' It shows a smooth and quick transition from one activity to the next.

content.focus_text: 他今天早上起床以后就听中文录音了。
content.focus_pinyin: Tā jīntiān zǎoshang qǐ chuáng yǐhòu jiù tīng Zhōngwén lùyīn le.
content.focus_gloss_en: He listened to the Chinese audio right after he got up this morning.

grammar_points.pattern: 王朋看了电视以后就去睡觉了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wáng Péng kàn le diànshì yǐhòu jiù qù shuì jiào le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Wang Peng went to bed right after watching TV. '就' connects watching TV and going to bed.

grammar_points.pattern: 我做了功课以后就去朋友家玩儿。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ zuò le gōngkè yǐhòu jiù qù péngyou jiā wánr.
grammar_points.explanation_en: I will go to my friend's place right after I finish my homework. '就' indicates the immediate plan after finishing homework.

content.notes: Pattern: Action 1 + 以后 (yǐhòu) + 就 (jiù) + Action 2

grammar_points.pattern: 王朋看了电视以后就去睡觉了。

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Simultaneous Actions with 一边...一边...
segments.teaching_goal: To teach the 'yìbiān... yìbiān...' structure for describing two actions happening at the same time.

narration_track.subtitle_en: How do you say you're doing two things at once? You use the structure [zh:一边]...[zh:一边]. This is equivalent to saying 'while' or 'at the same time' in English. Li You uses it twice in her diary. First, she says [zh:我一边吃饭，一边听录音], 'I ate while listening to the recording.' Later, she says [zh:我们一边吃，一边练习说中文], 'We ate while practicing speaking Chinese.' The structure is simple: Subject, then [zh:一边] plus the first action, followed by [zh:一边] and the second action. It's perfect for describing multitasking!

content.focus_text: 他常常一边吃饭一边看电视。
content.focus_pinyin: Tā chángcháng yìbiān chī fàn yìbiān kàn diànshì.
content.focus_gloss_en: He often eats and watches TV at the same time.

grammar_points.pattern: 我一边洗澡一边唱歌。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ yìbiān xǐ zǎo, yìbiān chàng gē.
grammar_points.explanation_en: I sang while I was showering. Both actions happen simultaneously.

grammar_points.pattern: 我妹妹喜欢一边看书一边听音乐。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ mèimei xǐhuan yìbiān kàn shū, yìbiān tīng yīnyuè.
grammar_points.explanation_en: My younger sister likes to listen to music while she reads. This describes a habitual preference for doing two things at once.

content.notes: Pattern: Subject + 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 1, 一边 (yìbiān) + Action 2

grammar_points.pattern: 我一边洗澡一边唱歌。

segments.teaching_goal: To explain how to string verbs together to describe a sequence of actions, where the order of verbs matches the order of actions.

narration_track.subtitle_en: In Chinese, you can often list a series of actions by simply putting the verbs one after another, without needing words like 'and' or 'to'. The order of the verbs follows the natural sequence of the events. For instance, Li You says she will [zh:到图书馆去上网], which literally means 'arrive at the library, go, and surf the web.' The verbs [zh:到], [zh:去], and [zh:上网] are linked together to describe the whole process. Another example is [zh:王朋来找我打球], 'Wang Peng came to find me to play ball.' The actions happen in that exact order: come, find, play.

content.focus_text: 下午我要到图书馆去看书。
content.focus_pinyin: Xiàwǔ wǒ yào dào túshūguǎn qù kàn shū.
content.focus_gloss_en: This afternoon, I will go to the library to read.

grammar_points.pattern: 他常常去高小音家吃饭。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā chángcháng qù Gāo Xiǎoyīn jiā chī fàn.
grammar_points.explanation_en: He often goes to Gao Xiaoyin's place to eat. The sequence is 'go' then 'eat'.

grammar_points.pattern: 我明天想找同学去打球。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ míngtiān xiǎng zhǎo tóngxué qù dǎ qiú.
grammar_points.explanation_en: I'd like to find some classmates to play ball with tomorrow. The sequence is 'find classmates', 'go', then 'play ball'.

content.notes: The verbs are ordered chronologically: 到 (arrive), 去 (go), 看 (read).

segments.segment_title: Grammar: The Particle 了 (le) in a Series
segments.teaching_goal: To teach that when listing a series of completed actions, 了 (le) is usually placed only after the final verb in the series.

narration_track.subtitle_en: We've learned that [zh:了] can mark a completed action. But what happens when you have a list of completed actions? In Chinese, you typically don't put [zh:了] after every single verb. Instead, you place it at the end of the series. Look at this long sentence from the diary describing the Chinese class. The teacher taught pronunciation, new words, grammar, and how to write characters. Finally, she 'gave' them a new text. The particle [zh:了] appears after [zh:给], the final action in that list: [zh:还给了我们一篇新课文]. This makes the sentence flow much more naturally than repeating [zh:了] over and over.

content.focus_text: 老师教我们发音、生词和语法，也教我们写字，还给了我们一篇新课文。
content.focus_pinyin: Lǎoshī jiāo wǒmen fāyīn, shēngcí hé yǔfǎ, yě jiāo wǒmen xiě zì, hái gěi le wǒmen yì piān xīn kèwén.
content.focus_gloss_en: The teacher taught us pronunciation, vocabulary, and grammar, also taught us to write characters, and also gave us a new text.

content.notes: Notice '了' (le) appears only after the final verb in the series, '给' (gěi).

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the verbs in the sentence. Show '了' appearing only after the last verb, while the others remain without it.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: The Particle 的 (de) with Adjectives
segments.teaching_goal: To explain when to use 的 (de) between an adjective and a noun.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's discuss the particle [zh:的], which is often used to connect an adjective to a noun. The rule depends on the adjective. For adjectives with two or more syllables, like [zh:有意思] (interesting), you usually need [zh:的]. For example, [zh:有意思的电影] means 'an interesting movie.' However, for single-syllable adjectives like [zh:新] (new), you often don't need [zh:的]. In the text, we see [zh:新课文], 'new text.' But there's a catch! If you add [zh:很] (very) before the adjective, you must use [zh:的], even for single-syllable ones. For example, you would say [zh:很大的教室] for 'a very big classroom'.

content.focus_text: 漂亮的学校 / 新课文 / 很大的教室
content.focus_pinyin: piàoliang de xuéxiào / xīn kèwén / hěn dà de jiàoshì
content.focus_gloss_en: beautiful school / new text / very big classroom

grammar_points.pattern: 漂亮的学校
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: piàoliang de xuéxiào
grammar_points.explanation_en: Use '的' with a two-syllable adjective like '漂亮' (piàoliang).

grammar_points.pattern: 新课文
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: xīn kèwén
grammar_points.explanation_en: Omit '的' with a one-syllable adjective like '新' (xīn).

grammar_points.pattern: 很大的教室
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: hěn dà de jiàoshì
grammar_points.explanation_en: Must use '的' when '很' (hěn) precedes the adjective, even a one-syllable one like '大' (dà).

content.notes: Usage of 的 (de) depends on the adjective's length and the presence of 很 (hěn).

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: piàoliang de xuéxiào
grammar_points.explanation_en: Use '的' with a two-syllable adjective like '漂亮' (piàoliang).

grammar_points.pattern: 新课文
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: xīn kèwén
grammar_points.explanation_en: Omit '的' with a one-syllable adjective like '新' (xīn).

grammar_points.pattern: 很大的教室
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: hěn dà de jiàoshì
grammar_points.explanation_en: Must use '的' when '很' (hěn) precedes the adjective, even a one-syllable one like '大' (dà).

segments.visual_notes: Show three columns illustrating the rules: '2+ Syllable Adj + 的 + Noun', '1-Syllable Adj + Noun', and '很 + Adj + 的 + Noun'.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Ongoing Actions with 正在 (zhèngzài)
segments.teaching_goal: To teach the 'zhèngzài + verb' structure to indicate an ongoing action, and its relationship with 在 (zài).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Our final grammar point is about describing an action that is currently in progress. To do this, you use the word [zh:正在] before the verb. It's like adding '-ing' to a verb in English. In the diary, when Li You arrives at Bai Ying'ai's dorm, she finds that [zh:她正在做功课], 'She was in the middle of doing her homework.' The word [zh:正在] emphasizes that the action is happening right at that moment. You can also use just [zh:在] for the same purpose, but [zh:正在] is a bit more emphatic, really stressing the 'right now' aspect of the action.

content.focus_text: 我昨天到他宿舍的时候，他正在练习发音。
content.focus_pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān dào tā sùshè de shíhou, tā zhèngzài liànxí fāyīn.
content.focus_gloss_en: When I got to his dorm yesterday, he was in the middle of practicing pronunciation.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 李友，你在做什么？ A: 我在练习写汉字。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ zài zuò shénme? Wǒ zài liànxí xiě Hànzì.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Here, '在' (zài) is used to indicate an ongoing action. It's slightly less emphatic than '正在' (zhèngzài).

grammar_points.pattern: 我们现在正在上课，你别打电话。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen xiànzài zhèngzài shàng kè, nǐ bié dǎ diànhuà.
grammar_points.explanation_en: '正在' (zhèngzài) strongly emphasizes that the class is happening right now.

content.notes: 正在 (zhèngzài) + Verb = an action in progress.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 李友，你在做什么？ A: 我在练习写汉字。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ zài zuò shénme? Wǒ zài liànxí xiě Hànzì.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Here, '在' (zài) is used to indicate an ongoing action. It's slightly less emphatic than '正在' (zhèngzài).

grammar_points.pattern: 我们现在正在上课，你别打电话。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen xiànzài zhèngzài shàng kè, nǐ bié dǎ diànhuà.
grammar_points.explanation_en: '正在' (zhèngzài) strongly emphasizes that the class is happening right now.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a 'loading' or 'in progress' icon next to the verb when '正在' is on screen to visually represent the ongoing action.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key grammar patterns and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned in this lesson. We talked about placing time expressions before the verb, using [zh:就] for sequential actions, and [zh:一边...一边] for simultaneous actions. We also covered how to use [zh:了] in a series of verbs and when to use [zh:的] with adjectives. Finally, we learned to describe ongoing actions with [zh:正在]. Keep practicing these patterns, and you'll be able to describe your day in Chinese just like Li You!
